claims.claim_text: The June 2023 logistics-company outage narrative with an 80-node cluster, exact timestamps, a PSU failure chain, a 40-second `NotReady` transition, a 5-minute toleration window, and 23 unavailable pods.
claims.span_hint: lines 28-29 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: In June 2023, a logistics company running an 80-node bare metal Kubernetes cluster experienced a cascade failure that started with a single faulty power supply. At 2:47 AM, worker-34's redundant PSU failed. The server continued on its remaining PSU. Nobody noticed because there was no hardware-level alerting. At 3:12 AM, the second PSU tripped due to a power surge on the same circuit. Worker-34 went offline. Kubernetes marked the node as `NotReady` after 40 seconds and began rescheduling pods -- but only after the default 5-minute taint toleration expired. During those 5 minutes, 23 pods were unavailable.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A single hardware fault can escalate quickly when there is no hardware-level alerting and workloads stay bound to a failed node until default node-failure tolerations expire.
claims.rationale: This is a specific outage narrative with precise dates, timings, and impact counts, but no attributable source is provided.

claims.claim_text: The follow-on Ceph/rack cascade narrative with a 4 TB OSD, recovery saturation, and packet loss on neighboring workers.
claims.span_hint: line 30 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: Then it got worse. Worker-34 hosted a Ceph OSD with 4TB of data. The OSD went down, triggering Ceph recovery across the remaining OSDs. The recovery I/O saturated the storage network on the same rack. Worker-33 and worker-35, sharing the same top-of-rack switch, experienced packet drops. Their kubelet heartbeats became intermittent. The control plane marked them as `NotReady` too. Now three nodes were degraded, and Ceph was trying to rebuild 4TB of data across already-stressed nodes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If a failed node also hosts storage or participates in a busy replicated storage system, recovery traffic can stress the surrounding failure domain and turn one node failure into a wider availability problem.
claims.rationale: This paragraph reads as a real incident with exact infrastructure details and quantified storage state, but it has no source.

claims.claim_text: The on-call timeline, 45-minute manual response, 2.5-hour total incident, and $180,000 business-impact claim.
claims.span_hint: line 32 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: The on-call engineer was paged at 3:18 AM. By the time they logged in, diagnosed the problem, and manually intervened, 45 minutes had passed. The fix: power-cycle worker-34 from the BMC, wait for it to boot, and let Ceph rebalance. Total incident time: 2.5 hours. Revenue impact: $180,000 in delayed shipment processing.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Without automated remediation, on-call response and manual hardware recovery can stretch a node outage from minutes into a much longer incident.
claims.rationale: Exact outage durations and revenue-loss figures require a primary incident source; none is provided.

claims.claim_text: Automated remediation would have power-cycled within 2 minutes, a 60-second toleration would have sufficed, and the whole incident would have been resolved in under 5 minutes.
claims.span_hint: line 34 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: An automated remediation system would have detected the node failure within seconds, attempted a BMC power cycle within 2 minutes, and if that failed, fenced the node and triggered reprovisioning. The 5-minute pod eviction timeout could have been tuned to 60 seconds for non-stateful workloads. The entire incident could have been resolved in under 5 minutes without human intervention.
claims.suggested_rewrite: With automated remediation and workload-specific eviction settings, many node failures can be handled much faster and with less manual intervention.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is sound, but the exact recovery and eviction timings are illustrative rather than sourced.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes marks a node `NotReady` after 40 seconds by default.
claims.span_hint: line 76 | section: Node Failure Detection Architecture
claims.anchor_text: Kubernetes marks a node as NotReady only when the kubelet stops sending heartbeats -- which takes 40 seconds by default.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kubernetes marks a node unhealthy only after missed heartbeats accumulate for about 50 seconds by default.
claims.rationale: Current Kubernetes docs describe the default node-monitor-grace-period as 50 seconds, so the exact 40-second default is outdated.

claims.claim_text: Node Problem Detector is a DaemonSet that monitors system logs and reports problems as Kubernetes node conditions.
claims.rationale: The upstream Node Problem Detector repository documents log monitoring and export of problems to Kubernetes.

claims.claim_text: A custom `hardware-health-checker` should run every 60 seconds, watch EDAC and SMART, and flip `HardwareHealthy=False` after thresholds such as >100 correctable ECC errors.
claims.anchor_text: NPD supports custom health check plugins. Configure a `hardware-health-checker` that runs every 60 seconds, checking ECC errors (via `/sys/devices/system/edac/`) and SMART disk health (via `smartctl -H`). When errors exceed thresholds (e.g., >100 correctable ECC errors, or SMART health FAILED), the plugin sets a `HardwareHealthy=False` node condition that Machine Health Checks can act on.
claims.suggested_rewrite: NPD supports custom health check plugins. For example, you can add a custom health-check plugin that periodically inspects EDAC counters and disk SMART health, then sets a custom node condition when those checks fail.
claims.rationale: The custom-plugin capability is real, but the exact polling interval, thresholds, and condition name are local design choices rather than sourced defaults.

claims.claim_text: MachineHealthChecks watch node or machine health conditions and trigger remediation after configured unhealthy durations.
claims.span_hint: lines 140-142 | section: Machine Health Checks (Cluster API)
claims.rationale: Cluster API documentation defines MachineHealthCheck condition checks and remediation behavior.

claims.claim_text: Topology spread constraints can use rack labels to distribute pods across physical failure domains.
claims.span_hint: lines 400-401 | section: Topology Spread Constraints & Rack-Aware Scheduling
claims.rationale: The topology spread docs explicitly cover administrator-defined topology keys for spreading workloads across failure domains.

claims.claim_text: Ceph's CRUSH map can place replicas across rack-level failure domains so a single rack outage does not remove all copies.
claims.rationale: Official Ceph docs directly describe CRUSH failure domains and rack-aware placement, but `docs.ceph.com` is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Since Kubernetes 1.22, node-failure eviction uses taint-based eviction rather than the removed `--pod-eviction-timeout` flag.
claims.anchor_text: Since Kubernetes 1.22, pod eviction on node failure uses taint-based eviction rather than the removed `--pod-eviction-timeout` flag.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Node-failure eviction relies on taint-based eviction and per-pod `NoExecute` tolerations.
claims.rationale: The stable, sourceable behavior is taint-based eviction; the versioned statement about the removed flag is not cleanly backed here and is easy to get wrong.

claims.claim_text: When a node becomes `NotReady` or `Unreachable`, Kubernetes adds built-in taints and automatically gives pods 300-second tolerations unless overridden.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes taints and tolerations docs explicitly describe the built-in taints and the default 300-second tolerations.

claims.claim_text: Ceph recovery and backfill can materially reduce operational performance, so operators tune recovery/backfill settings to limit impact during rebuilds.
claims.span_hint: lines 455-464 | section: Tuning Storage Recovery Throttling
claims.rationale: Official Ceph docs describe backfill/recovery performance impact and the relevant tuning knobs, but `docs.ceph.com` is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Borg handles about 5 machine failures per day in a 10,000-machine cell, implying 0.05% daily failure and one node failure every 20 days in a 100-node cluster.
claims.anchor_text: - **Google's Borg system automatically handles about 5 machine failures per day in a typical 10,000-machine cell.** The failure rate is roughly 0.05% per day, which means in a 100-node bare metal cluster, you should expect approximately one node failure every 20 days. Design your remediation accordingly.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Large production schedulers are designed to absorb regular machine failures.** Even smaller bare-metal clusters should assume node loss is a routine event and automate remediation accordingly.
claims.rationale: The exact Borg numbers and derived arithmetic are unsourced in the module and should not be preserved as precise facts.

claims.claim_text: Machine Health Checks were inspired by AWS Auto Scaling Group health checks, and cloud replacement happens in seconds while bare-metal replacement takes minutes to hours.
claims.anchor_text: - **Machine Health Checks were inspired by AWS Auto Scaling Group health checks**, which automatically replace unhealthy EC2 instances. The Cluster API MHC brings the same concept to bare metal, but replacing a physical server takes minutes (reboot) to hours (reprovision) instead of seconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Machine Health Checks play a similar role to health-check-based replacement in cloud autoscaling systems**, but bare-metal recovery usually takes longer because rebooting or reprovisioning physical hardware takes time.
claims.rationale: The similarity is fair, but the historical inspiration claim and exact timing comparison are not sourced here.

claims.claim_text: ECC memory corrects single-bit errors and detects double-bit errors, and a Google study found about 8% of DIMMs see correctable errors per year while such DIMMs are 13-228x more likely to suffer a fatal uncorrectable error.
claims.anchor_text: - **ECC (Error Correcting Code) memory can correct single-bit errors and detect double-bit errors.** A Google study found that about 8% of DIMMs experience at least one correctable error per year, and DIMMs with correctable errors are 13-228x more likely to experience an uncorrectable (fatal) error. This is why monitoring ECC errors is critical for predicting failures.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **ECC memory can catch many memory faults, and recurring ECC alerts are a strong warning sign that a DIMM needs attention before it causes wider disruption.**
claims.rationale: This bullet mixes an over-broad ECC statement with a misquoted study result about fatal errors, so the safest fix is to keep the lesson and drop the false precision.

claims.claim_text: With `maxUnhealthy: 40%`, remediation still proceeds in a 10-node pool when 3 nodes are unhealthy.
claims.rationale: Cluster API health-check docs state remediation continues when the unhealthy count does not exceed the configured 40% limit.

claims.claim_text: At 4 unhealthy nodes out of 10, MHC stops remediating because 40% meets the threshold.
claims.anchor_text: If a 4th node fails while these are being remediated: 4/10 = 40%, which meets the threshold -> MHC **stops** remediating and alerts.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If a 5th node fails while these are being remediated: 5/10 = 50%, which exceeds the threshold, so MHC stops further remediation until the cluster is healthier.
claims.rationale: Current docs say remediation stops when the unhealthy count exceeds the configured limit, not when it merely equals 40%.

claims.claim_text: 5% spare capacity is an industry-standard bare-metal rule, so a 60-node cluster should use exactly 3 spares.
claims.anchor_text: - 5% of 60 = 3 spare nodes (industry standard for bare metal)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - A practical starting point is at least one spare per rack, then adjust based on utilization and recovery targets.
claims.rationale: The exact percentage and its presentation as an industry standard are heuristic, not a cited standard.

claims.claim_text: Kubelet client certificates rotate at 80% of their one-year lifetime.
claims.anchor_text: These certificates are auto-rotated by kubelet (default: 1 year validity, rotate at 80% lifetime)
claims.suggested_rewrite: These certificates are commonly issued for one year by default, and kubelet requests a replacement as expiration approaches
claims.rationale: Upstream docs describe a rotation window as expiry approaches, not a single exact 80%-of-lifetime trigger.

claims.claim_text: An expired kubelet client certificate can stop a rebooted node from rejoining the cluster.
claims.rationale: The kubeadm troubleshooting docs explicitly describe expired kubelet client certificates causing authentication and rejoin problems.

further_reading.title: Cluster API MachineHealthCheck
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary reference for remediation thresholds, unhealthy-condition timeouts, and short-circuit behavior.

further_reading.title: Node Problem Detector
further_reading.why_relevant: Upstream reference for log monitors, health-check plugins, and how node problems surface to Kubernetes.

further_reading.title: Taints and Tolerations
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains built-in node taints, default 300-second tolerations, and taint-based eviction behavior.

further_reading.title: Node Status
further_reading.why_relevant: Documents `Ready` and `Unknown` semantics and the current default node-monitor-grace-period.

notes: The local cold-start API failed (`api: FAILED to start — see logs/api.log`), so this seed was assembled from the module text, the shared section pool, and primary upstream docs. Current Kubernetes docs show a 50s default node-monitor-grace-period, not 40s. Ceph-specific CRUSH and recovery-throttling claims are real but need allowlist expansion for `docs.ceph.com`.
